Ellison's one of the most famous writers in history, but Bova's no slouch. He soon became editor of "Analog" where he was beloved and won the Hugo award for Best Professional Editor six times. This isn't one of his. He accepted this one on behalf of George R.R. Martin, whose career he started. George couldn't make it. He was running a chess tournament at the time. Writers used to have fun. Now we just complain about Twitter. On Twitter. "Brillo" was about the world's first robot police officer. His name's Brillo 'cause Brillo pads are metal fuzz. That's pretty good. This is one of the earliest stories in fiction about a robot cop-- a commonplace trope today, and soon in real life. It wasn't the first example. The most famous earlier one would be Isaac Asimov's "Caves of Steel." Remember this. It will come up later. Ellison and Bova thought the idea had legs and decided to adapt it into a TV show. They pitched the idea to a few companies including NBC where an executive named Terry Keegan said no. They later showed "Brillo" to Paramount where the head of development-- Terry Keegan-- he'd recently been hired there-- said no. The same man passed on the same pitch twice at two companies. transcript_snippet:
Every human used to have to hunt or gather to survive. But humans are smart...ly lazy so we made tools to make our work easier. From sticks, to plows, to tractors we’ve gone from everyone needing to make food to, modern agriculture with almost no one needing to make food — and yet, we still have abundance. Of course, it’s not just farming, it’s
everything. We’ve spent the last several thousand years building tools to reduce physical
labor of all kinds. These are mechanical muscles. Stronger, more reliable, and more tireless
than human muscles ever could be. And that's a good thing. Replacing human labor
with mechanical muscles frees people to specialize and that leaves everyone better off - even those
still doing physical labor. This is how economies grow and standards of living rise. Some people have specialized to be programmers
and engineers whose job is to build mechanical minds. Just as mechanical muscles made human
labor less in demand so are mechanical minds making human brain labor less in demand. This is an economic revolution. You may think
we've been here before, but we haven't. This time is different. ## Physical Labor When you think of automation, you probably
think of this: giant, custom-built, expensive, efficient, but really dumb robots blind to
the world and their own work. They were a scary kind of automation but they haven't
taken over the world because they're only cost effective in narrow situations. But they're the old kind of automation, this
is the new

transcript_snippet:
How long do
you think it will take before machines do your
job better than you do? Automation used to mean big stupid machines
doing repetitive work in factories. Today they can land aircraft,
diagnose cancer and trade stocks. We are entering a new age of automation
unlike anything that's come before. According to a 2013 study,
almost half of all jobs in the US could potentially be automated
in the next two decades. But wait; Hasn't automation
been around for decades? What's different this time? Things used to be simple. Innovation made human work
easier and productivity rose. Which means that more staff
or services could be produced per hour using the same
amount of human workers. This eliminated many jobs, but also
created other jobs that were better which was important because the
growing population needed work. So, in a nutshell, innovation,
higher productivity, fewer old jobs, and many
new and often better jobs. Overall, this worked well for a majority
of people and living standards improved. There's a clear progression in
terms of what humans did for a living. For the longest time,
we worked in agriculture. With the Industrial Revolution, this
shift into production jobs and as automation became more widespread,
humans shifted into service jobs. And then only a few moments ago in human
history, the Information Age happened. Suddenly, the rules were different.
Our jobs are now being taken over by machines much faster
than they were in the past. That's worrying of course..

I'm going to become a plumber. Jeffrey Hinton is the Nobel Prize winning pioneer whose groundbreaking work has shaped AI and the future of humanity. Why do they call it the godfather of AI? because there weren't many people who believed that we could model AI on the brain so that it learned to do complicated things like recognize objects and images or even do reasoning. And I pushed that approach for 50 years and then Google acquired that technology and I worked there for 10 years on something that's now used all the time in AI. And then you left. Yeah. Why? So that I could talk freely at a conference. What did you want to talk about freely? How dangerous AI could be. I realized that these things will one day get smarter than us. And we've never had to deal with that. And if you want to know what life's like when you're not the apex intelligence, ask a chicken. So there's risks that come from people misusing AI. And then there's risks from AI getting super smart and deciding it doesn't need us. Is that a real risk? Yes, it is. But they're not going to stop it cuz it's too good for too many things. What about regulations? They have some, but they're not designed to deal with most of the threats. Like the European regulations have a clause that say none of these apply to military uses of AI. title: Debugging Life | Ep. 147 - The 8 Worst Technology Flops of 2025
description_head:
A retrospective look at the biggest tech failures of 2025 reveals eight major products and initiatives that failed to meet expectations. This list provides a cautionary tale for investors and developers about the risks of overhyped technologies that lack practical utility.

📍 Chapters:
00:00 - Intro
transcript_snippet: It's the sheer velocity of the hype cycle that really gets me. We've seen this before, obviously, the dot bubble, the 3D TV craze of 2010, the initial VR surge, but 2025 was different. It was the year the AI everything bubble didn't just leak. It essentially experienced a rapid unscheduled disassembly. If you look at the historical data, specifically the capital infusion into these eight specific projects, it's staggering how much was lost on what were essentially solutions looking for problems, right? And it wasn't just the money. It was the collective face pal of the entire tech consuming public. I remember when the AuraLink headband first hit the market early in the year. The marketing was everywhere. They promised it would use non-invasive neurosensors to synchronize your Spotify playlist with your actual emotional state. It was supposed to be the soundtrack of your soul. The Neurolinker. Yes. Manufactured by Cognitech. From a historical perspective, they were trying to capitalize on the 2024 biohacking trend. But the technical reality was a disaster. The sensors were incredibly sensitive to sweat. If you were working out, which is when most people want a soundtrack, the salt would interfere with the electrodes. Oh, it was worse than that. I tried one for a week. I'd be at the gym trying to get hyped on some heavy metal, but because I was slightly stressed about a deadline, the headset would decide I needed meditative rainforest sounds. You're trying to bench press 300 lb

title: Saranyaa Parthikumar - AI: Overhyped Job Replacement?
description_head:
Is AI really coming for all our jobs? Or is that just the hype? 🤔

In this episode, Saranyaa Parthikumar cuts through the noise on one of the biggest questions in tech right now, and her take is grounded and refreshing.

AI can automate tedious tasks and boost productivity, but it can't replace huma
transcript_snippet: What is one AI idea you think is overhyped right now? There are many but I think uh the most fearful one is that AI is coming for all our jobs and AI is going to replace humans. I mean initially there was lot of buzz about but what I've realized is um lot more factors play into when AI can replace a human and in what capacity can AI replace a human like for example these large language models and then the genai tools they are very good in crunching information that is already available so for example think before internet if you were in a particular city if you need to get an information you either go and talk to scholars. Next step you go and pour into the books that are available in the library. And then after the internet era, you started searching the internet. And then after the internet boom, internet became more than whatever the scholar can give you, right? Like I'm going and asking this researcher or like PhD some information and then the internet can give me information of 10 p.m. that is how the internet evolution worked. And now with LLMs, I think LLM can give you the information of 100 PhDs, but then it can only give information that is already present for like out ofthe-box thinking for some of the things that require decision making with creativity. I'm not talking about generating a video or generating an ad campaign, but think where a creative decisioning is needed. For example, a child is crying and then a mother can come up with 10 different games to pacify

This is the reality we're heading toward with apo voice avatars. However, there's a crucial issue that many overlook. As we embrace these digital identities, do we truly understand the implications of our voices being replicated and modified? Picture this. You're scrolling through social media and suddenly you hear a voice that sounds just like yours delivering a speech. But it's not you. It's an ignorated version expressing thoughts you never shared. This technology could lead to serious misunderstandings or even identity theft. The lines between who you are and who you could become are blurring in ways that might change your life forever. Now imagine having fun with a voice avatar creating a persona that speaks multiple languages. But what happens when that voice is used to make decisions affecting your reputation? You might think that could never happen to me, but let's consider the implications. A voice can evoke emotions, build trust, and influence opinions. What if someone misuses your voice avatar without your consent? The potential chaos is staggering. Companies using this technology aren't just creating tools, fire shaping identities behind every voice you hear online. Are you truly in control of your digital self? title: ALEXA'S $2B SECRET: Your Smart Speaker Is Spying on You
description_head:
The legal landscape of 2026 has just shifted. In this episode of "The Verdict: Court News," we analyze three landmark decisions that shatter the status quo of Big Tech and State lines.

Inside the files:

THE DIGITAL GHOST: The Robin Williams estate just won a massive victory against Elysium AI. Why
transcript_snippet: The era of digital resurrection has hit a massive legal wall. Just hours ago, a federal court in California issued a nationwide preliminary injunction against Allesium AI Studios. The judge ruled that their Legend series, which features a hyperrealistic digital clone of the late Robin Williams, violates the newly established federal right to publicity. This isn't just about copyright. It's about digital identity. The court stated that a person's voice and persona are not just data. They are an extension of their personality that survives death. For years, Elysium AI operated in a gray zone, using thousands of hours of Williams private recordings and film outtakes to train their model for a series of interactive AI comedy specials. But today, Zelda Williams and the actor's estate won a decisive victory. Zelda's statement released just minutes after the verdict was clear. My father's soul is not a subscription service. Judge Sarah Evans delivered a 45-page opinion that sent shock waves through Silicon Valley. She wrote, "The dead do not lose their humanity simply because they can be reduced to binary code. To simulate a person's creative spark without consent is not innovation. It is digital graverobing." The injunction effectively halts the distribution of any AI model that hasn't been vetted by the heirs. Silicon Valley is in a state of absolute panic. Tech leaders fear that any voice that sounds too similar to a known figure could now trigger a multi-million dollar lawsuit.
label: YES

title: AI Robots Are Replacing Office Workers! 🤖💼 | The Future Is Here! | #ai #robot #officework #aiworks
description_head:
AI Robots Are Replacing Office Workers! 🤖💼 | The Future Is Here!

#ai #artificialintelligence #robot #officework #airobot #aiworks #AIRobots #OfficeWork #Automation #FutureOfWork #TechInnovation  #OfficeWork #FutureOfWork #TechNews #AI2025 #Automation #RobotsInOffice

_______________________________
transcript_snippet: Tired of the same old office grind? Let's talk about robots taking over. Picture this. AI robots handling your boring tasks like scheduling meetings, managing emails, and even crunching numbers. These digital assistants are not just a sci-fi dream anymore. They're here in our offices making our lives easier. Imagine walking into work and instead of dreading the mountain of paperwork, you actually get to focus on the fun stuff, creative projects, and brainstorming sessions. AI can analyze data faster than you can say spreadsheet. And before you panic, no, they're not here to replace you. Think of them as your new office buddies, taking care of the mundane so you can shine at what you do best. So next time you're buried under emails, just remember your robotic helper is just around the corner. Welcome to the future of office work, ready to embrace it. From simple automation to advanced machine learning, AI has come a long way. Today, AI is not just a tool, but a partner in the workplace. It helps in decision-m, enhances productivity, and even predicts future trends. Imagine a world where your AI colleague can suggest the best course of action based on data analysis. The future is now, and it's exciting. AI is not here to replace us, but to work alongside us. By taking over repetitive tasks, AI allows us to focus on what truly matters, innovation and creativity. Think of AI as a team member who never gets tired and is always ready to assist. Together, we can achieve more than we
label: YES

transcript_snippet: Hoy está de moda hablar de sostenibilidad, pero nadie habla de qué tipo de sostenibilidad. Así que hoy les vengo a contar sobre la sostenibilidad industrial. ¿Qué está pasando con la sostenibilidad en la industria? La inteligencia artificial de carácter generativo cambió completamente las reglas del juego. Hoy en día, una agencia de publicidad, un equipo de marketing puede diseñar piezas a una escala y velocidad inmensa, ya que la inteligencia artificial generativa le permite crear textos, imágenes o videos a unas escalas muy rápidas. Esto está redefiniendo la industria como la conocemos, pero no solo la redefine en eficiencia o personalización de los contenidos para el público, la está redefiniendo en un sentido del que nadie está hablando, y es que verdaderamente el uso excesivo e indiscriminado de IA generativa está generando una enorme huella de carbono a nivel industrial. Hoy cada vez que hablamos de contenido generado por inteligencia artificial y lo usamos, estamos generando una enorme huella de CO2E. Y esto en la publicidad se multiplica, ya que la publicidad es mucho de prueba, error y siempre producir más, más contenidos para todo tipo de canales digitales. Pero pasa algo, la publicidad habla de sostenibilidad y la publicidad que habla de sostenibilidad no es sostenible en sus propios procesos. Hoy vengo a hablarles sobre una posible solución o mejor dicho una regulación. Vengo a presentarles el modelo GAA, gobernanza adaptativa, inmunológica, artificial. Pero, ¿qué
